behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Real git rename detection output must be parsed correctly. Git's diff-rename feature produces specific output format (e.g., 'similarity index' lines) that requires subprocess execution to test. A mock that returns simplified 'renamed' status would hide parsing failures and version-specific edge cases in how git reports renames. Only real git repositories with actual rename operations (git mv) expose these failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with file rename operations using git mv (e.g., rename file1.py to renamed1.py). Commit the rename. Run the tool. Parse output and verify: (1) renamed files appear exactly once in a 'renamed' category, (2) they do not appear as both 'deleted' and 'added', (3) total counts (add+delete+rename+modify) match the actual operations performed.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary file handling depends on real subprocess output from git, which may contain null bytes, unprintable characters, and OS-specific encoding issues. Mocking could test basic 'doesn't crash' logic but would miss encoding edge cases, signal handling, and buffer management with actual binary content. Real binary files in a real git repository (images, compiled binaries, archives) expose failures that mocks hide.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with actual binary files (e.g., PNG image, compiled executable, PDF document, zip archive). Stage and commit these files. Run the tool. Verify: (1) tool exits successfully without crash or exception, (2) binary files are listed and counted in output, (3) output shows correct counts (binary files should not be treated as deleted if present in HEAD), (4) no encoding errors or garbled characters in stdout.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: For a CI tool, testing against real git in actual no-commit state (git init without commits) is the realistic minimum. While Integration-level mocking could test error-handling logic, real git version differences, initialization quirks, and subprocess invocation edge cases require System-level testing. Different git versions may return different error messages or exit codes that the tool must handle correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new git repository using git init without adding any files or commits. Run the tool against this no-commit repository. Verify: (1) tool exits with success status (exit code 0), (2) stdout contains a valid empty report (all counts are zero), (3) output format is consistent with normal successful runs, (4) stderr is empty or contains no error messages.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires validation against actual git history as ground truth. A mock git output could test parsing logic in isolation, but cannot validate correctness of counts against real change history. Off-by-one errors, edge cases in change detection, and complex multi-operation sequences are only caught by testing against real repositories with known change sequences. The failure—count mismatch—is invisible to mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with a predetermined sequence of changes: commit 1 (add 5 files), commit 2 (delete 2 files), commit 3 (modify 3 files), commit 4 (rename 1 file). Run the tool. Parse the output report and verify exact match: 5 additions, 2 deletions, 3 modifications, 1 rename (total 11 changes). Cross-validate independently using git log --name-status and git diff-tree commands.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Git's staging system produces different subprocess output depending on whether changes are staged (git add) or unstaged (working directory). A mock could return different outputs for each state, but would miss subtle differences in how git actually formats staged vs unstaged change reports. Only real git state transitions with real subprocess output can test whether the tool correctly handles both modes and produces identical summaries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with initial commit and tracked file. Make modifications to the tracked file. Stage some changes using git add. Run the tool and capture output A. Reset staging (git reset) and modify the same file identically to produce the same net changes. Run the tool again and capture output B. Verify outputs A and B are byte-for-byte identical (diff should be empty). Repeat with multiple files and operations to ensure consistency.
